meta.title: SF-SPAA-5: Account Governance, Cost Control, and Recovery Administration

questions.stem: Which organization-level role can create and manage accounts in a Snowflake organization?
questions.explanation:
ORGADMIN operates at the organization scope for organization-wide administration including account management. The other roles are account-scoped or not appropriate.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/organizations-manage

questions.stem: Which command changes the active role for the current Snowflake session?
questions.explanation:
`USE ROLE` changes the current session role. The other forms are not the standard command for this task.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/use-role

questions.stem: A company wants separate billing, administration, and security boundaries for two business units. What is the best high-level design?
questions.explanation:
Separate accounts provide stronger administrative and billing boundaries than just schemas, databases, or warehouses inside one account.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/intro-key-concepts

questions.stem: Which system-defined role is primarily associated with creating and managing users and roles?
questions.explanation:
USERADMIN is the built-in role focused on user and role management. ACCOUNTADMIN is broader and more privileged than necessary.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: What is a primary use case for account parameters?

options.text: Configuring account-wide behavior such as session and security defaults
Reducing warehouse size automatically after every query

questions.explanation:
Account parameters control account-wide behavior and defaults. The other options are unrelated or too specific.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/intro-key-concepts

questions.stem: Which feature helps analyze usage across multiple accounts in the same Snowflake organization?
questions.explanation:
Organization usage views provide cross-account visibility for an organization. The other items are not organization-level usage reporting features.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/organization-usage

questions.stem: What is a failover group used for?

options.text: Replicating supported objects and enabling disaster-recovery failover

questions.explanation:
Failover groups support replication and disaster recovery for supported objects. The other answers describe different capabilities.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/account-replication-intro

questions.stem: Which statement best reflects the principle of least privilege in Snowflake administration?

options.text: Grant ACCOUNTADMIN to every analyst to avoid blockers
Grant only the minimum roles and privileges needed for each job function
Assign SYSADMIN to all service users by default
Avoid roles and grant privileges only to individual users permanently

questions.explanation:
Least privilege means granting only the required access for each responsibility. The other options overprivilege users or scale poorly.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: A new account must use an enterprise naming convention and common policies from day one. Which administrative approach helps most?

options.text: Standardize provisioning and baseline configuration at account creation time
Rely only on each team to configure settings later
Disable RBAC until all projects are onboarded

questions.explanation:
Standardized provisioning and baseline configuration create consistency from the beginning. The other options delay or weaken governance.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/intro-key-concepts

questions.stem: Which role should generally be used sparingly because it combines broad administrative powers across the account?
questions.explanation:
ACCOUNTADMIN is highly privileged and should be tightly controlled. The other roles do not represent the same broad authority.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of a reader account?

options.text: To allow consumers without their own Snowflake account to access shared data
To replace virtual warehouses for compute

questions.explanation:
Reader accounts let providers share data with consumers who do not manage a full Snowflake account. The other options are unrelated.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-sharing-reader-create

questions.stem: Select TWO statements that are generally true about Snowflake account architecture. (Select TWO)

options.text: Databases and warehouses are separate objects so storage and compute can scale independently
Roles are central to RBAC-based administration
Virtual warehouses store table data permanently
Users query data only through organization-level roles

questions.explanation:
Snowflake separates compute from storage and relies heavily on roles for RBAC. Warehouses do not store persistent table data, and users do not query only through organization roles.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-overview

questions.stem: A company needs business continuity for a set of databases plus some account objects in another region. Which Snowflake feature family is most relevant?
questions.explanation:
Replication and failover features address cross-region business continuity. The other features do not provide full disaster-recovery coverage.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/account-replication-intro

questions.stem: Why might an administrator use tags at the account level?

options.text: To classify objects for governance, discovery, and policy-driven management
To force every query into result cache

questions.explanation:
Tags help organize and govern data assets through classification and policy workflows. They do not tune warehouse hardware or create users.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/object-tagging

questions.stem: Which object scope is most appropriate for setting a policy that should apply to every session in the account unless overridden more specifically?
questions.explanation:
An account-level setting is the broadest common administrative scope for default session behavior. The other scopes are too narrow or unrelated.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/intro-key-concepts

questions.stem: What is the primary purpose of a resource monitor?

options.text: To track and control credit consumption with thresholds and optional actions

questions.explanation:
Resource monitors are used for credit governance and can notify or suspend usage based on thresholds. The other answers describe different features.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/resource-monitors

questions.stem: Which warehouse property automatically reduces cost by stopping idle compute after a defined period?
questions.explanation:
AUTO_SUSPEND stops an idle warehouse after a configured interval, helping reduce unnecessary spend. The other parameters serve different purposes.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-overview

questions.stem: What does multi-cluster warehouse scaling primarily help address?
questions.explanation:
Multi-cluster warehouses are used to handle concurrency bursts by adding compute clusters. They do not solve compliance, replication, or identity tasks.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-multicluster

questions.stem: A dashboard workload has many short, repetitive queries. Which Snowflake capability may reduce repeated compute work when the exact same query result is still valid?
questions.explanation:
Result caching can serve repeated identical queries when the cached result remains valid. The other features do not optimize repeated query execution.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/querying-persisted-results

questions.stem: Which view family is most useful when investigating historical query execution details such as elapsed time and scanned bytes?

options.text: WAREHOUSE_EVENTS is the only possible source

questions.explanation:
Query history views are the standard source for historical execution diagnostics. The other options are narrower and not the primary query-performance source.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/ui-query-profile

questions.stem: What is a likely reason to resize a warehouse upward temporarily?

options.text: To provide more compute for heavier workloads or faster completion

questions.explanation:
Warehouse size controls available compute resources. The other options are unrelated to warehouse sizing.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-overview

questions.stem: Which statement about warehouse suspension is correct?

options.text: Suspending a warehouse deletes its cached data files permanently from tables
Suspending a warehouse stops compute charges for running clusters until resumed
Suspending a warehouse removes RBAC grants
Suspending a warehouse disables replication globally

questions.explanation:
Suspending a warehouse stops active compute billing for that warehouse until it resumes. It does not delete table data, remove grants, or disable replication globally.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-overview

questions.stem: A team experiences long queue times even though individual queries are not very expensive. What is the most relevant first hypothesis?

options.text: Warehouse concurrency is insufficient for the workload pattern
Time travel retention is too high
The account has too many tags

questions.explanation:
Long queue times often point to concurrency pressure on the warehouse rather than per-query complexity. The other choices are weak fits for the symptom.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-multicluster

questions.stem: Which administrative action best supports chargeback by business unit?

options.text: Apply tags or naming standards and analyze warehouse and query usage by those groupings
Store all data in one temporary schema

questions.explanation:
Consistent tagging or naming paired with usage analysis supports cost attribution. The other actions either harm governance or do not enable chargeback.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: Select TWO actions that commonly help control warehouse cost. (Select TWO)

options.text: Use resource monitor thresholds and actions
Increase every warehouse to the largest size by default

questions.explanation:
Auto-suspend and resource monitors are standard cost controls. Defaulting everything to the largest size increases cost, and disabling history does not solve spend management.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-overview

questions.stem: Why might an administrator inspect warehouse load metrics over time?

options.text: To understand utilization, concurrency, and whether a different size or multi-cluster setting is justified
To convert permanent tables to transient tables

questions.explanation:
Load metrics help determine whether warehouse sizing and scaling settings match workload behavior. The other answers are unrelated.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-multicluster

questions.stem: What is the main difference between scaling up and scaling out for warehouses?

options.text: Scaling up changes warehouse size; scaling out adds clusters for concurrency
Scaling up adds users; scaling out removes users
Scaling up changes RBAC; scaling out changes object ownership

questions.explanation:
Scaling up increases per-cluster compute capacity, whereas scaling out adds clusters for higher concurrency. The other answers are false.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-multicluster

questions.stem: Which privilege model is central to Snowflake security administration?

options.text: Discretionary ACLs only with no roles
Mandatory access control by sensitivity level only

questions.explanation:
Snowflake security is built primarily around RBAC using roles and grants. The other models are not the core mechanism.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: What does `OWNERSHIP` on a Snowflake object imply?

options.text: The role can manage the object and ownership transfer semantics apply
The role can query the object but not alter it
The role can only view object history

questions.explanation:
OWNERSHIP is a powerful privilege that confers control over the object and affects grant management. It does not simply mean read-only access.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: Which role is commonly associated with managing grants and security objects?
questions.explanation:
SECURITYADMIN is the built-in security-focused administrative role commonly used for grants and security object management.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: A table contains sensitive salary data that should appear masked to most analysts but visible to a privileged payroll role. Which feature is most appropriate?
questions.explanation:
Masking policies allow conditional obfuscation of sensitive fields based on role or context. The other options do not implement column masking.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-column-intro

questions.stem: Which statement about future grants is correct?

options.text: They help automatically grant privileges on new objects created in a schema or database
They retroactively fix ownership conflicts only
They are available only for network policies
They replace the need for roles

questions.explanation:
Future grants automate permissions on objects created later in a given scope. They do not replace roles or apply only to network policies.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: What is the safest general approach when granting privileges to end users?

options.text: Grant privileges to individual users directly whenever possible
Grant roles to users and privileges to roles
Grant ACCOUNTADMIN to all service accounts
Avoid secondary roles entirely in every design

questions.explanation:
Best practice is typically to grant privileges to roles and then assign roles to users. The other options reduce manageability or overprivilege users.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: A provider wants to give another Snowflake account read access to selected datasets without handing over internal admin access. Which feature is most relevant?
questions.explanation:
Secure data sharing is built for sharing data across accounts without broad administrative access. The other features solve different problems.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-sharing-intro

questions.stem: Which command family is used to inspect effective grants on an object or role?
questions.explanation:
`SHOW GRANTS` is the standard way to inspect grant information. The other `SHOW` commands inspect different object types.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: Select TWO statements that correctly reflect secure view behavior and governance. (Select TWO)

options.text: Secure views are designed to protect underlying implementation details better than regular views in sharing scenarios
Access to a secure view still depends on proper privileges
Secure views automatically grant OWNERSHIP to all consumers
Secure views replace the need for masking policies in every case

questions.explanation:
Secure views help hide underlying details and still require correct privileges. They do not auto-grant ownership or eliminate all other governance controls.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/views-secure

questions.stem: A team wants row-level restrictions so regional managers see only records for their region. Which Snowflake feature is designed for that purpose?
questions.explanation:
Row access policies enforce row-level filtering rules. The other features do not govern row visibility.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-row-intro

questions.stem: Why should administrators be careful with `WITH GRANT OPTION`?

options.text: It allows the recipient to further delegate privileges, expanding access paths
It encrypts the object keys twice
It is required for all read access
It converts a role into a user

questions.explanation:
Grant option lets recipients regrant privileges, which can broaden access unexpectedly if not controlled. The other answers are false.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: What is a common reason to use a custom role hierarchy?

options.text: To align privileges with organizational job functions and simplify delegated administration

questions.explanation:
Custom role hierarchies help map job functions and administration boundaries cleanly. They do not disable system roles or replace schemas.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: What is the primary purpose of a network policy in Snowflake?

options.text: Restricting allowed client IP addresses for authentication

questions.explanation:
Network policies restrict which client network locations can authenticate. The other features are unrelated.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies

questions.stem: Which feature should an administrator use to require an additional verification factor for interactive users?
questions.explanation:
MFA adds a second factor to strengthen interactive authentication. The other options are not authentication controls.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/admin-security-mfa

questions.stem: A company wants private connectivity so traffic between Snowflake and its cloud environment does not traverse the public internet. Which concept is most relevant?

options.text: Private connectivity options such as PrivateLink or equivalent cloud private endpoints

questions.explanation:
Private connectivity features support private network paths between Snowflake and cloud environments. The other objects are unrelated.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/private-link

questions.stem: What is a key reason to rotate keys or credentials for service integrations?

options.text: To reduce exposure if secrets are compromised and follow security hygiene
To increase result cache hit rate

questions.explanation:
Credential rotation reduces the impact of leaked or stale secrets and is standard security hygiene. The other options are unrelated.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/key-pair-auth

questions.stem: Which statement about SSO is most accurate?

options.text: SSO centralizes authentication with an identity provider and can improve user access management
SSO is only for reader accounts
SSO can be enabled only on weekends

questions.explanation:
SSO integrates Snowflake with an external identity provider for centralized authentication. It complements rather than replaces authorization controls like RBAC.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/admin-security-fed-auth

questions.stem: When troubleshooting blocked sign-ins after a network policy change, what should an administrator verify first?

options.text: Whether the client IP range is allowed by the applicable policy

questions.explanation:
A network policy issue most directly depends on whether the source IP is permitted. The other items do not explain an authentication block caused by the policy.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies

questions.stem: Select TWO controls that most directly strengthen access security for interactive users. (Select TWO)

options.text: SSO with a trusted identity provider

questions.explanation:
MFA and SSO directly improve authentication security. Warehouse size and VARCHAR length do not.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/admin-security-mfa

questions.stem: Why might an administrator choose key-pair authentication for a service user?

options.text: To support non-interactive authentication without passwords in some automation patterns
To reduce storage cost for tables

questions.explanation:
Key-pair authentication is commonly used for service or automation accounts. It does not replace compute or network governance.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/key-pair-auth

questions.stem: What is the best description of data encryption in Snowflake?

options.text: Snowflake provides encryption for data at rest and in transit as part of the platform
Customers must implement all encryption manually for every table
Encryption is available only for Enterprise accounts and above in transit

questions.explanation:
Snowflake encrypts data at rest and in transit as part of the managed service. The other statements misrepresent the platform.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-encryption

questions.stem: A security review asks for evidence of who accessed sensitive objects. Which data source is most directly relevant?

options.text: Access history and related account usage views

questions.explanation:
Access history helps investigate object access patterns and is directly relevant for auditing sensitive usage. The other items are not primary access-audit evidence.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/access-history

questions.stem: What is zero-copy cloning primarily used for?

options.text: Creating fast copies of objects for dev/test or recovery workflows without duplicating underlying storage immediately
Permanently archiving data outside Snowflake only

questions.explanation:
Zero-copy cloning creates metadata-based copies that reuse underlying storage until changes occur. The other options are unrelated.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/object-clone

questions.stem: Which table type is often chosen when lower-cost storage is desired and fail-safe is not required?
questions.explanation:
Transient tables omit fail-safe and can reduce storage-related retention cost in appropriate scenarios. The other objects are different feature types.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-failsafe

questions.stem: What is Time Travel used for?

options.text: Querying or restoring historical data versions within the retention period

questions.explanation:
Time Travel allows historical access and recovery within retention windows. The other options are unrelated.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-time-travel

questions.stem: Which statement about Fail-safe is correct at a high level?

options.text: It is an additional recovery period after Time Travel for permanent objects, managed by Snowflake
It is a warehouse scaling mode
It applies only to temporary tables
It lets end users undo queries themselves instantly

questions.explanation:
Fail-safe is a Snowflake-managed recovery mechanism after Time Travel for supported permanent data. The other answers are inaccurate.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-failsafe

questions.stem: A data set is large, mostly read-heavy, and queries often filter on a small subset of columns. Which physical concept most directly supports pruning efficiency in Snowflake storage?
questions.explanation:
Snowflake uses micro-partitions and their metadata for pruning and efficient scan reduction. The other items are unrelated to storage pruning.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/tables-micro-partitions

questions.stem: What is a likely use case for search optimization service?

options.text: Improving selective point-lookups or highly selective queries on large tables

questions.explanation:
Search optimization can improve highly selective lookups on large tables. It is not a replacement for security, warehouse control, or replication.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/search-optimization-service

questions.stem: Which operation creates an independent logical copy of a database at a point in time for experimentation?
questions.explanation:
Cloning is the standard point-in-time copy mechanism for experimentation or recovery workflows. The other actions do not create a copy.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/object-clone

questions.stem: Why might an administrator use a transient schema for staging workflows?

options.text: To avoid fail-safe storage overhead for data that does not need that protection
To require MFA for all queries

questions.explanation:
Transient objects can be appropriate for lower-retention staging data that does not need fail-safe. The other answers are unrelated.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-failsafe

questions.stem: Select TWO statements that are generally true about warehouses and storage. (Select TWO)

options.text: Virtual warehouses provide compute, while data storage is separate from them
Suspending a warehouse does not delete the data stored in databases
Warehouse size changes the fail-safe retention window
Databases stop existing when their warehouse is suspended

questions.explanation:
Snowflake separates compute from storage, and suspending a warehouse does not affect persisted database data. The other statements are false.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-failsafe

questions.stem: A table is accidentally modified, and the team wants to restore it to an earlier state within retention. Which feature is the first place to look?
questions.explanation:
Time Travel is the primary built-in feature for reverting or querying earlier table states inside the retention window. The other choices are unrelated.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-time-travel

questions.stem: Which feature most directly helps administrators review sign-in activity?
questions.explanation:
LOGIN_HISTORY provides authentication-related event visibility. The other objects are not the primary login-audit source.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/access-history

questions.stem: What is a strong reason to centralize audit monitoring?

options.text: It helps detect anomalous behavior, support investigations, and demonstrate compliance more consistently
It removes the need for RBAC

questions.explanation:
Centralized audit monitoring improves security operations and compliance evidence. The other claims are unrelated or false.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/intro-key-concepts

questions.stem: A regulator asks for evidence that privileged roles are tightly controlled. Which practice is best evidence?

options.text: Documented role design, limited assignment of powerful roles, and periodic access reviews

questions.explanation:
Governed role assignment and regular reviews are strong evidence of privilege control. Warehouse size and deleting history do not help.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/intro-key-concepts

questions.stem: Which data source is most relevant when proving that a masking or row-access policy was part of the governance design?

options.text: Object definitions, grants, and policy attachments in Snowflake metadata

questions.explanation:
Policy attachments and metadata about governed objects provide evidence of implemented governance controls. The other items do not show policy design.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-column-intro

questions.stem: Select TWO activities that fit a compliance-focused Snowflake administration practice. (Select TWO)

options.text: Regular review of access and audit history
Using governance tags and policies on sensitive data
Granting ACCOUNTADMIN broadly so auditors have fewer questions
Disabling history retention to reduce evidence volume

questions.explanation:
Compliance practice commonly includes reviewable audit trails plus data classification and policy controls. Broad ACCOUNTADMIN grants and deleting evidence are poor practices.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: Why is immutable or durable audit evidence important in a governance program?

options.text: It supports trustworthy investigations and external reviews of who did what and when
It eliminates the need for user training

questions.explanation:
Trustworthy audit evidence is essential for investigations and compliance reviews. The other choices are unrelated benefits that audit data does not provide.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/intro-key-concepts
